text: Cool, alright, ready to go. Last week we did linear regression and you did some cool projects.
Today we're going to cover logistic regression and we're starting a new fun project for you all to work on.
Yeah.
So we covered regressions where the goal was to predict continuous values.
Now, sometimes the output that we want to predict is not a continuous variable, and for the majority of the time it's mostly classification.
Regression studies do exist, but most of what I see is classification.
Even if it's just the outcome where you are predicting between two quantities, or if you are doing something advanced, for instance, object detection.
There, you have certain predefined categories that you have to predict something for in an image.
Segmentation as well—you either have to declare a pixel to be belonging to some object or a background.
So most of what I see is classification in machine learning, and that's what we are going to cover today.
The basic building block, the simplest algorithm that we have in order to do classification, is called logistic regression.
So let's build the case for why logistic regression is a good algorithm for it and why we cannot make use of the existing linear regression algorithm in order to do that.
Okay, I've written these things in advance because it takes time and the loss function for logistic regression is a bit complicated, so I have it in advance here.
Anyway, so here what you see is called the hypothesis function of logistic regression.

Previously, for linear regression, the hypothesis function was this thing: in order to produce an output, you have to take the input, multiply it with weights, and then add the bias term in order to produce an output.
That was linear regression.
Now, in order to do logistic regression, we take that output and then plug it inside this function, which is called the sigmoid function.
The question is: why do we do that? Why can't we make use of linear regression?
So let's see why this is not a good idea.
So, for instance, take this hypothetical example where we have two classes.
These red circles here represent the first class—let's call it the zero class.
And then these crosses represent the second class, so let's give it the label of one.
Let's see what happens if we try to fit a linear regression model to it in order to do classification, to decide between whether something belongs to the zero class or the one class.
Just as an example, we would take the case where if we have, let's say, a tumor of some size.
The idea is: if the tumor is smaller in size, it is a benign tumor, and if it grows in size, it becomes a malignant tumor.
This is just an example.
So, on the x-axis, you can consider this to be the size of the tumor, and on the y-axis, these are the two possible outcomes.
It can be a benign tumor, in which case the output is zero, or it could be a malignant case, in which case the output is one.
This makes sense, right? I'm not confusing you?
Yeah, so we want to make a model that will detect if a tumor is malignant or benign based on how big it is, exactly.
So what you see here—these red circles and crosses—this is just some historical data.
It shows that if the tumor is lower in size, there's a greater chance that it's a benign tumor, it's not malignant.
But if it grows in size, then it becomes a malignant tumor.
And we want to take a linear regression, or in other words, a straight line to fit to this data.

We want to model this data, and the reason is that in the future we would like to have a model where we can just provide it the size of the tumor, and it should be able to tell us if it's a malignant tumor or benign tumor.
Now, as we are aware, a linear regression line does not yield zero or one; it yields a continuous output.
So it starts from zero and can go up to any number based on what the input is.
So how can we convert this into a classification case where it has to produce either a zero or a one?
What we can simply do is put a threshold, so you can say that if the output is greater than this quantity, which could be anything, for instance...
It's 0.5. Let's say if linear regression produces something that is greater than 0.5 we would declare it as one, and if it produces something that is lower than 0.5 we would predict this to be zero.
I'm just putting 0.5 here because I have written here zero and one, but it could be anything.
You can pick this threshold to be anything for the reason that linear regression does not yield an output between 0 and 1.
It could be between 0 and any positive number, but the idea is that you just pick a threshold above which you declare that it's a 1 and below which you declare it is a 0.
So this can work. We can make use of linear regression in order to do this.
By the look of it, it seems it can work, but the problem is there are certain problems regarding linear regression.
First thing is it is producing something that is not between 0 and 1.
It would be producing something that would be a lot greater than that.
Well, we just need between 0 and 1, so that's one thing, but we can ignore that problem for a second.

Second problem with using linear regression as a classification model is suppose this was the original data we had with these red circles, but now we introduce something like there's another example that is now introduced.
This is another extra example whose size is let's say this much, and it's a malignant tumor.

Of course, because after a certain size all tumors are going to be malignant for the simple reason that if a tumor of this size is malignant, increasing the size further would definitely mean that it's also a malignant tumor.
So there's no point in changing your model.
If there are certain examples that appear on the far right, the model should stay the same.
It should not affect the model itself.
The problem with linear regression is if you introduce this and you retrain this algorithm using linear regression.
If you remember, the goal of linear regression was to reduce the square differences.
In this case, if this is the newer example and that was the previous linear regression line that we had trained, the difference that the model

would be producing from the true output would be this much, and remember that this difference gets squared, so it's even a huge error; it becomes even more bigger and definitely such a bigger loss would affect this line if you retrain it.
So the line would tend to be tilted towards this newer example because of this huge difference.
That's why if you retrain this linear regression algorithm once again, this green line is what you'll get.
And you can see that there's a significant difference between the blue line, which was earlier, and this green line, which is after retraining.
Why?
Because it is trying to reduce this error.
Now we have this much error, and in order to do that, it's also increasing these errors as well.
Previously this was the error, but now for the green line that error is increased, even though it should also be closer to these points.
But the point is that was so much bigger of a difference that it introduced errors in these as well.
And still the overall error between that one and this one is smaller.
So if you're confused about this discussion, just ignore it.
But the point is if you introduce points towards the far right, the linear regression line tends to cover that because it is reducing the square differences.
Now, what is the consequence of that for our strategy to use this as a classification model?
Well, the consequences are that previously we picked this threshold to be 0.5, and as a result of that, anything that was lying on this side of the line we declared as benign tumors.
And anything that was occurring on that side of the line we declared as malignant tumors.
Now, picking the same threshold here, you can see that the threshold that we picked now cuts the line at this point.
So anything that is occurring on this side of the line are now declared as benign tumors, and anything that are on that side of the line are declared as malignant tumors.
So the problem that you can see here is because of this extra introduction of this example on the far right, the threshold that we picked earlier is now classifying these two points incorrectly.
They're declaring that these are benign tumors even though these are
Malignant tumors, and that's because we have introduced something that is way far more on the right side, and intuitively we know that it should not be something that it should be doing.
Just like I said, if something is of a certain size, anything greater than that size is definitely a malignant tumor, so it should not affect the model.
But linear regression, as you can see, is getting affected by this outlier example that we have introduced.
Does this make sense?
Yeah, makes sense. Perfect.
So for this reason we need to come up with a model which is not a straight line but is rather doing something that looks like this.
Let me change the color; I have introduced a mess here, but I'll try to—okay, so we need a model that looks like this, somewhat like this at least.
So it is flat on the left side, it is flat on the right side, and as you increase the size, it makes this jump as the tumors start becoming malignant tumors with the increasing size.

So for this model, it doesn't really matter if you introduce an example here or here or even there—it would always be producing an output of one if you keep on increasing the size of the tumor, and it would always be producing the output of zero if a tumor is of a very lower size.
So we need a model of this sort, a curve of this sort, which fits this data and not a straight line, right?
Okay, so the question is, how—a little bit of like a transistor or something like that?
Transistor, yeah. I think the threshold after which the current starts flowing.
Exactly. Yeah, that technology works somehow.
But the question is, how do we go from this straight line to that curve?
Well, we can make use of something called a sigmoid activation function or sigmoid function, which is a nonlinear function which looks like this.
So you take the output, plug it inside this negative exponent, and the output that it produces squashes the output between zero and one.
So whatever the value of Z is, this activation function or this function is going to produce either a zero or a one.
or something in between, in which case it produces, let's say, a zero in cases where Z is negative.

So if you take a computer and create a numpy array of some negative values and positive values and then you plug them inside this function and then you plot them, you'll see that for the values of Z which are larger negative values, this line asymptotes at zero, and for the values of Z that are positive, as you increase them, it asymptotes at one.

Here, if you plug in exactly zero for Z, what happens? So if you plug in e to the 0, which is 1, 1 divided by 1 plus 1 is 1 divided by 2, which is 0.5. So for Z equal to 0, it is producing exactly 0.5.
Anything on the left side starts going towards zero; anything on the right side starts going towards one. That's exactly what we require.
So we need a function which squashes the output of this between zero and one, because that's what we are required to have here.
The tumor could be either a zero or a one; it still is producing something in between.
It's not like it's producing a zero or a one, but it will be something in between.
But what we can do is then pick a threshold. So let's say you pick a threshold of 0.5. We say anything that is lower than 0.5 is zero, and anything that is greater than 0.5 is one.
This threshold picking is manual, so you can change this.
You normally can plug this to be 0.5, but if it is something of higher importance, like if it's something very critical, you can even decrease this.
So you can say, even if there's a chance of three percent, I would like this to be one, and if it is lower than that, I would like it to be zero.
So this threshold picking is your choice.
Any questions so far?
No? Okay. So the rest of the story then is just what we've been doing for linear regression.
Now we have this model. We have these parameters, so this is parameterized by this W and B, and we need to come up with a way to figure out the values for W and B.
Well, initially, because we randomly initialize W and B, whatever this model is producing would be way off from what the true output is.
And in order to compute that difference, we need our cost function or loss function.
So for linear regression, the loss function was
In MSE, we simply take the differences, square them, and then accumulate them across all the examples, and that's our cost function.
Well, it turns out that the MSE loss function you cannot make use of it here.
The question is, why can we not make use of it?
The answer is because we have introduced this nonlinearity here, and because of that nonlinearity, if you now try to plot the loss function, let me see if I have plotted—yes, here.
If you now plot the loss function J against any of the parameters—let's say this is w—the loss function now looks like this.
While previously, in the linear equation, if you remember, the loss function looked something like this.
Now, can anyone tell me what is the problem with this red loss function, which is non-convex?
Felix, any thoughts?
Okay, so the problem with this non-convex loss function is that you see here we have a lot of what are called local minima.
Previously, for this convex loss function, we just had a single minimum point at this—for this entire loss, just this one—and it was called global minimum.
Global minimum means this is the lowest point; we don't have any local minimas.
Now, what is the problem with these local minimas?
The problem with these is: suppose initially when you started, the random initialization for w was, let's say, this, and you begin at this point.
Remember how we update the values of w—we make use of gradient descent.
If you remember the equations for gradient descent, w was updated like this, right?
What this was doing is computing the derivative of the cost with respect to w.
What was the derivative? It was nothing but the slope of the tangent line drawn at that point.
So, making use of gradient descent, we gradually reduce the value for the loss function.
Now, what happens if you have these local minimas is that if you reach at this point and you compute the derivative at this point, it would be a perfectly horizontal line—the tangent.
A line will be perfectly horizontal.
What is the slope of a perfectly horizontal line?
It is zero.
So if it is zero and you take that zero and you plug it in here, this entire equation, this part becomes zero, so W is equal to W, meaning no updates on W.
So once you reach this point, the loss is no longer getting updated for the simple reason that the gradient here is zero and you are stuck at this local minimum.

So while looking at the loss function, you can confuse yourself into thinking that this is the loss point because it's no longer getting updated, but if you plot it, you'll see that it's not the minimum point.
It's a local minimum.
The minimum point on this plot is, let's say, this one, that's where I should be reaching, not this one, because that's the global minimum.
But the problem is, how do we do that if our loss function looks like this, and for this simple reason, we cannot make use of MSE loss function because it results in this non-convex loss?
It is because of the reason that we have introduced the sigmoid function, which is highly nonlinear.
The question is, how do we mitigate this issue?
Well, we can make use of a different loss function, which is called cross-entropy loss.
The cross-entropy loss looks something like this.
It looks scary, but you'll see shortly it's a very simple loss function.
But if you plug in this loss function, you then effectively make your loss function from non-convex.
You make it again a convex loss function.

Now if you ask me why plugging in a cross-entropy loss function converts it from non-convex to convex, the proof of that comes from a concept in probability and statistics called maximum likelihood estimation.
I haven't personally looked into the details of it.
There is a long proof which shows that if you have a sigmoid activation function and you use a cross-entropy loss, the non-convex cost function becomes convex.
Of course, you're not getting into those proofs, but it comes from probability and statistics.
You can study it there by the name of maximum likelihood estimation.
Estimation, but let's take a look at this loss function.
What is it doing?
So if you carefully look at this loss function, there are two parts to it: this is the first part, and then there is the second part.
Why do we have two parts?
Well, it turns out that these are the two parts that we have combined.
So we make use of a loss term when the true output is one, and this when the true output is zero.
Let's take the first one: negative log of Y, H.
How is this accumulating the loss?
What happens if my model is producing something different than what the true output is, which is one?
How would this accumulate the loss?

So if you take a computer and plot a log function—so you take some amount, some array, and compute the log of it, and then invert that by negating it by putting a negative sign there—the resultant plot would look somewhat like this.
And because our model is producing something only between zero and one, we are not taking a look at a range outside it.

We are only interested in something which is between zero and one, for the simple reason that our Y H, which is the output of the model, would be producing something that could be either zero or one or something in between.
And if you take this array and plot negative log of those values, you see a plot that looks like this.
I'm not confusing you people.
Okay, so let's see: I said that if the true output is one, we make use of this loss function, which looks like this.
Let's see what happens if the true output is one.
What happens if my model is producing something closer to zero?
You can clearly see if the model is producing something closer to zero—let's say this—while the true output is one, this loss is going to be very high.

So it would be something like this: the more you get closer and closer to zero, the more higher the loss appears, because the true output is one, the model is producing something very close to zero, which is not something it should be doing.
That's how it is computing that cost.
Now, if the model is producing something closer to one and the true output is also one, the loss is going to be very minimum.
If it is producing perfectly one, the loss is going to be exactly zero.
Example: that's how it is accumulating the loss that is for when the true output is one.
What happens if the true output is zero?
If the true output is zero, then we make use of this loss function.

It turns out if you plot this, the resultant loss plot looks like this: if the true output is zero and the model is producing something closer to one, the loss is going to be very high because it will be very far from what the true output is.
And if the model is producing something closer to zero while the true output is also zero, the loss is going to be minimum.
It is going to be closer and closer to zero.
So we have these two parts which accumulate the loss from the function, but we cannot plug in separate parts.
We need to somehow put these two into a single equation which we can call our loss function.
It turns out this is the equation which would account for both of them.
Now let's take a look at what happens and how it is combining these two different parts.
So this Yi here that you see is the true output that comes from the data.
In this y hat here is the prediction of the model.
This i here is simply representing the i-th example.
So we are summing over all the examples.
m is the total number of examples in our data.
So let's see what happens if the true output is zero.
If the true output is zero, we would be plugging zero here, right, because this is the true output.
If you plug in zero here, this whole term boils down to zero.
And this is also the true output—if you plug in zero here, 1 minus 0 is 1.
One multiplied with this thing is log of 1 minus y hat, which is exactly what this thing is: negative log of 1 minus y hat when the true output is zero.
And when the true output is one, you plug in one here.
This term boils down to this, and one minus one, so this term becomes zero.
And if this becomes zero, this entire portion, which is what that first part was doing here when the true output was one, combines.
So that's how we combine these two parts to make this entire loss function.
We simply sum over all the examples and take the average of it by dividing it with M.
This makes sense.

It would not be producing that, right?
It would be some small negative, some small number.
If it is on the zero side, it's not going to be perfectly zero.

In that case, it's true that the loss would be infinite, but it's not going to happen because that blue line in the loss curve, or in the sigmoid curve that you have, that blue line, the logistic curve, never fully touches it, but it never reaches that exact one value or exact zero value.
So it can always be adjusted to be a bit closer.
It can go closer and closer, but you'd never see something that would be exactly a one or exactly a zero.
For one, it would be something like 0.99999, that's the maximum that it can reach.
You won't see a perfect one in any case, right?

You saw that we make use of the same model gradient descent to do that.
So in order to update the value for w, let's say you take the previous value of w and subtract from it alpha times the derivative of the loss function with respect to w.
And for the second one, the bias term, you do the same.
This is the case where you just have one feature.
What happens if you have multiple features?
So if you have five features, or
10 features, you just simply do the same operation, just differ the weight that you're looking into.
Suppose you're updating the weight for the third feature, so W3 would then become W3 equal to W3 minus Alpha times the derivative of the loss function with respect to W3.
If you do it for the fourth feature, you just simply change that, and the rest of the story stays the same.
So with that, basically what you're saying is we can add multiple logistic functions for multiple input variables as much as the function remains one.
So we still make use of just the sigmoid function.
Only the W's and the Z function.
The Z function you can add, so Z is equal to: if let's say we are looking into the cancer example, and let's say you are just looking into the size of the tumor, so that's just one feature.
In that case, size would be, let's say, X, so the Z would be Z equal to W times X plus B.
Now suppose you also are looking into the texture of the tumor, in that case Z would become equal to W1 times X1, which is the size, plus W2 times X2, which is the texture, plus B.
So the Z changes, but then you check the same Z and plug it inside the sigmoid, so the sigmoid stays the same.
Make sense?

It depends on how you define your labels.
Let's say our model is trying to detect cancer.
You could also say your model's trying to detect a healthy person, so then that would just flip everything, but let's say we're trying to detect cancer.
A true positive would mean they had cancer and our model detected it.
A false positive would mean they didn't have cancer but our model said they did.
A false negative would be they did have cancer but the model said they didn't.
A true negative would be they didn't and the model said they didn't.
Understanding all of these is important, depending on what you're trying to do.
This is often visualized in a confusion matrix, normally visualized in a prettier way.
Knowing all of these values is really useful.
Knowing that our model had no false negatives but it had one false positive when we tested it is interesting.
Accuracy, as Felix described it originally, is all of the true positives and the true negatives, whatever was correct out of all of the predictions.
There's precision, where when the person had cancer, how often did the model predict that correctly, completely ignoring all of the people that were healthy.
Recall is when the person did have cancer, how often did the model correctly predict that, completely ignoring all of the healthy people.
There's something called
F1 score, which is just a combination of all of these, but the main point here is that it's not just accuracy.
Depending on what you're looking at, because if it's not just accuracy, then a model that always predicted everyone was healthy would be 99% correct.
It's really a matter of understanding all of these values here.
